questions.question: Explain the purpose of ftok() function and how it generates a unique key for IPC.
keyPoints: ftok() converts pathname and project ID to IPC key
Same pathname and proj_id generate same key
Used by msgget, shmget, semget to access same IPC facility
If file is deleted and recreated, key may not match

questions.question: Explain the difference between Message Queues and Shared Memory in System V IPC.
keyPoints: Message Queues - kernel-managed message passing, structured messages with types
Shared Memory - direct memory access to common region, fastest IPC
Message Queues provide synchronization implicitly, Shared Memory requires explicit sync
Message Queues have size limits, Shared Memory limited only by system

questions.question: What are the operations performed by semop() function? Explain with example.
keyPoints: semop() performs atomic operations on semaphore set
Negative operation = wait (P operation) - decrements semaphore
Positive operation = signal (V operation) - increments semaphore
Zero operation = wait until semaphore reaches zero
Operations are atomic - no interruption between multiple semaphore ops

questions.question: Write the structure of message buffer used in System V Message Queues.
keyPoints: Message must have long mtype as first field
Data is stored in char mtext[] or char mtext[size]
User defines message structure with mtype as first member
mtype must be positive integer used for message selection
Example: struct msgbuf { long mtype; char mtext[100]; };

questions.question: Explain the use of IPC_CREAT and IPC_EXCL flags in IPC functions.
keyPoints: IPC_CREAT creates IPC facility if it doesn't exist
IPC_EXCL causes function to fail if facility already exists
Used together ensures new IPC facility is created
Without IPC_EXCL, returns existing facility if it exists
Usually combined with permission bits: IPC_CREAT | IPC_EXCL | 0666

questions.question: What is the purpose of shmat() and shmdt() functions in Shared Memory operations?
keyPoints: shmat() attaches shared memory segment to process address space
shmdt() detaches the attached segment from process
shmat() returns pointer to attached memory, used for read/write
shmdt() called when process finishes using shared memory
Segment removed only when all processes detach or explicit IPC_RMID

questions.question: Write a C program using System V Message Queues where sender process sends a message and receiver process receives it. Explain all steps.
keyPoints: Define msgbuf structure with mtype and mtext
Use ftok() to generate key, msgget() to create queue
Sender: msgsnd() to send message with positive mtype
Receiver: msgrcv() to receive message with matching mtype
Use IPC_NOWAIT for non-blocking or handle blocking
Use msgctl() with IPC_RMID to remove queue
Include error handling for all system calls

questions.question: Explain System V Shared Memory mechanism with necessary system calls. Write a program to demonstrate shared memory between parent and child processes.
keyPoints: Use shmget() to create shared memory segment
Use shmat() to attach memory to process address space
Process reads/writes to shared memory directly
Use shmdt() to detach when done
Use shmctl() with IPC_RMID to remove segment
Parent creates, child inherits or uses same key
Demonstrate data sharing between processes

questions.question: Explain System V Semaphores and demonstrate how they can be used for process synchronization with a program.
keyPoints: Semaphores are counting synchronization primitives
semget() creates semaphore set with specified number
semop() performs P(wait) and V(signal) operations
semctl() controls semaphore (initialize, get/set values)
Use negative value for wait, positive for signal
Common use: protect critical section / shared resource
Demonstrate mutual exclusion or producer-consumer

questions.question: Compare the three System V IPC mechanisms: Message Queues, Shared Memory, and Semaphores. Give advantages and disadvantages of each.
keyPoints: Message Queues: Kernel-managed, structured messages, automatic sync, limited size, slower
Shared Memory: Fastest IPC, direct access, requires explicit sync, race conditions possible
Semaphores: Pure synchronization, counting mechanism, cannot store data, used with other IPC
Message Queues - good for infrequent, structured data
Shared Memory - good for frequent, large data exchange
Semaphores - essential for synchronization, used with shared memory
